comments.content: What was the live-in occult college Dion Fortune discusses her experiences from in Psychic Self-Defense?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Good question. I don't happen to own a biography of her so I can't tell you.

replies.title: occult "college"
replies.content: According to Wikipedia, "Her first magical mentor was the Irish occultist and Freemason Theodore Moriarty.[38] She had befriended him while still involved in psychotherapy, believing that he could help one of her patients, a young man who had been fighting on the Western Front and claimed to be plagued by unexplained physical phenomena. Moriarty performed an exorcism, claiming that the young man was the victim of the soul of a deceased East European soldier which had latched onto him as a parasite.[39] Fortune became an acolyte of Moriarty's Masonic-influenced lodge, which was based in Hammersmith,[40] and joined his community of followers living at Gwen Stafford-Allen's home in Bishop's Stortford.[41] Moriarty spent much time talking about the lost city of Atlantis, a topic that would also come to be embraced by Fortune.[42] Fortune later fictionalised Moriarty as the character Dr. Taverner, who appeared in a number of short stories first published in 1922, later assembled in a collected volume as The Secrets of Dr. Taverner in 1926.[43] Like Moriarty, Dr. Taverner was portrayed as carrying out exorcisms to protect humans from the attacks of etheric vampires.[44]"

replies.title: planets
replies.content:
In my Wiccan training on the use of planetary sigils, I was taught that you never call the "spirit" of the planet, but instead you invoke the "intelligence" of the planet. The spirit is not evil, but unpredictable. The intelligence is more predictable in its action. These different entities have different names, but the usual tables of information don't contain this instruction. This particular lore was lost or deliberately abandoned in some Wiccan lineages--those that distained anything that seemed to be ceremonial magic and influenced by Jewish or Christian concepts.

Rita

replies.title: Re: planets
replies.content: That's an old and wise bit of Renaissance astrological magic. If you're evoking planetary fices, yes, you always want to call on the intelligence and ask for its help. As for the usual tables of information not having that, I'm sorry to hear it's dropped out of Wiccan tables -- you can find it all laid out in great detail in books on ceremonial magic, my book Circles of Power among them.

replies.title: Re: planets
replies.content:
As far as I have seen, the entire set of sigils and lore about using planets for magic dropped out of use in some lineages, not just the spirit vs. intelligence part. We learned about calculating planetary hours, but not much about why you would need to. That seems to me to be one of the main problems with Wiccan and wiccanate practice. With no unified set of practices to teach, what one learns depends so much on the talents and interests of one's teacher. If your HPS is into astrology, everyone learns to cast their horoscope, if she is interested in herbs you get a workshop in tinctures, and so on. There are bits of information in the Book of Shadows that may be given to a student with "we got this from our upline, no one really explained it, but we promised to pass it on." If anyone could unify all the bits it could be a powerful system--but given the splits and jealousies between different traditions and different lines within traditions, that will never happen.

Rita

replies.title: Re: planets
replies.content: That makes sense -- and it's unfortunate that so often people just study with one teacher.

replies.title: Re: Various
replies.content:
Here, a classic case of scientism for your viewing rage.

Recently, a book came to my knowledge: Primary Perception: Biocommunication with Plants, Living Foods, and Human Cells by Cleve Backster. The book is out of print, and the available copies command very high prices. So I made the mistake of consulting Wikipedia on the author. This part made my blood boil:

Backster's "Primary Perception" theory was a subject of the Discovery Channel television show MythBusters.[19] After all human and environmental stimuli that could alter the results were removed, the Mythbusters team reproduced Backster's experiments with the dracaena plant, yoghurt, saliva and eggs. They performed some of these experiments using a model of polygraph machine identical to the one used by Backster in his original research, while other experiments were performed with an EEG instrument, which is based on a different technology than the polygraph. Experiments where the team employed the same model of polygraph machine used by Backster showed positive results with the plant reacting both to actual harm, as well as thoughts of harm. In another segment of the episode, the team tested with an EEG by connecting it to a plant to check whether it would "see" eggs being catapulted randomly into boiling water. The EEG instrument registered no change in the plant and the myth was considered as "busted" in that particular segment of the episode. The differences between the two technologies likely played a key role in the differing results.[20]

Debunked! Isn't television wonderful? However, for a change, Wikipedia states that the different instruments were "likely" responsible for the negative result.

replies.title: Re: Various
replies.content:
That last segment of the Mythbusters operated under the assumption that the plants used have vision as we do, know what boiling water is, that the eggs used contained a living embryo etc, etc, and so would react with horror to this 'grisly act', in other words that they have a psychology and perceptions like ours. This doesn't make my blood boil so much as it makes me laugh my head off. Well, yeah of course there was 'no change'. Their experiment was essentially worthless and proved nothing one way or the other.

The writers of Wikipedia are no doubt somewhat aware of this, hence the weasel word 'likely'.

Anything on the Discovery Channel does not come under the rubric of 'scientific' and that includes Mythbusters. It's all about the ratings, not promoting real science in any way. Any further research on Backster's theories will require people who can think 'outside the box' and are not under the thumb of corporate backers. The return of Natural Philosophy is badly needed, I think, to revive the current flaccid state of research and understanding.

JLfromNH/Amber Insouciant Porpoise

Good evening, and thanks very much for hosting this space and your generous answers to so many questions every week.

1) Mostly for the commentariat, most likely: a friend of mine responded very well to some JMG books I recommended and is now asking about spiritual practices. He's going to get started with The Way of the Golden Section, but religiously, his tastes run Roman, so does anyone have recommendations for prayer practices, sources for religious ritual, or the like for Roman polytheism? I've already pointed him to hellenicfaith.com and recommended Marcus Aurelius, Epictetus, and Publius Syrus as likely candidates for meditation themes, but any further recommendations would be most welcome (he has a decent reading/writing knowledge of Latin, if that helps). neptunesdolphins, might you have some thoughts?

2) This may be more psychological, but I wonder if there might be an occult dimension: recently, while journaling or meditating on some things related to my spiritual practice, I've had some experiences of very strong grief/sadness. What is odd about these experiences is that they don't come with any clear knowledge of what they are "about" - I start feeling sad, I ask myself "what am I sad about?" or "might I be sad about X" and I don't really get an answer, just the feeling of sadness itself. This has happened both when I'm alone in my house and with others closer (like lying in bed next to my wife), and it doesn't feel like a particularly "alien" feeling, so I'm less inclined to think I'm picking up on someone else's feeling. If it's relevant, I tend rather strongly towards an Air way of handling things, and Water is often characteristically weak for me, and this is something I've been exploring. Any thoughts on how to figure out what this might be telling me besides more prayer, meditation, journaling, and returning to the OSA work I've let lapse?

3) This is only indirectly magical, and certainly not medical: a recent meditation pointed me rather forcefully at the heartbeat as an image, and I'd like to spend some time paying attention to it while meditating. Unfortunately, even when I'm fairly relaxed and paying good attention to my breath, my heartbeat is mostly not perceptible to me (unless I put my hand on my breast or throat or wrist, or when my heartrate is elevated). So, is it common to not be able to feel/hear your heartbeat when trying to focus on it? Are there any exercises, possibly similar to the OGS Solar Plexus exercise, I might try?

4) You've talked about how "superstition" literally means "things held over", things that had a rationale at some point, but now folks do without understanding. When it comes to magical or quasi-magical practices, do you believe that superstitions can be re-vitalized? For example, "knock on wood" is something done/said mindlessly by lots of folks, but if you intend to invoke the wood spirits in the piece of furniture, might you more out of what has become "superstition" to most folks?

5) You answered a question about the altar for the Order of the Golden Section last MM, and it got me wondering about multiple altars. Currently, I have a permanent altar for prayer, and a temporary altar I set up for Druid Grove rituals. If and when I follow the initiation in the OGS, it sounds like another temporary altar, set up on the edge of a space when needed, would work fine, but might you (and the commentariat) please share if you have any practical tips on managing multiple altars for different purposes?

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Roman Polytheism
replies.content:
1) On Roman Polytheism, I've found the essays of Horatius M. Piscinus to be a great source of obscure info, though they can be a little overwhelming and too detail heavy. Neopolytheist's blog is a great introduction for a beginner and I recommend starting there.

For literature, Cato's On Agriculture, Varro's On the Latin Language in 25 Books, Varro's Agricultural Topics in 3 Books and Macrobius' Saturnalia will be where you want to start hunting for flecks of gold.

And now for my personal religious views, which should be taken with a single molecule of sodium chloride and thrown out forthwith if you have a different interpretation:
The Roman Gods are not Greek Gods, though they might be called twins or close siblings. Many gods which appear to be "imported" are in fact the Romans projecting their own gods onto cultures they admired and were probably a little jealous of, though the Romans did import many foreign gods such as Aesculapius, though there was often a distinct "this is a Greek god, so we must worship him with the Greek Rite, while this Roman god represented with a greek statue and with Greek myths grafted on is Roman, so we use the regular Roman Rites for him."
Second, there's a lot of confusion about who the Lares are, since later Roman authors refered to multiole beings. I treat "Lares" as a catch-all term like "spirits" and use them primarily to refer to the spirits of the house (lares domestici), the spirits of the land and neighbourhood (lares compitales) and the spirits of the roads and crossroads (lares viales) and I call the spirits of my ancestors just "ancestors" instead of lares familiaris to decrease confusion. Also, while some scholars believe that every man has a guardian Genius and every woman has a guardian Juno, other scholars disagree with this interpretation and so do I, so I consider Genius to be the term for the gusrdian of every person and every piece of land (much less confusing). Your friend may find these artificial distinctions I've made useful, or not. Just remember that like the Romans saw a statue of Athena and said "Look, a statue of Minerva! Let's bring it home and build a temple to Minerva!" these are mostly my projections, influenced by my own desires and cultural artifacts, trying to make sense of a convoluted system mostly lost to time and unable to be entirely perceived by flimsy human intellects anyway.

Here are some links to get started:

Absolute Beginner:
https://romanpagan.wordpress.com/household-shrine-and-ritual/

One of the first Horatius M. Piscinus essays I read. Every essay is gold, though don't be afraid to discard specific practices that are too inconvenient (like using spring water or water gathered from rivers in the four cardinal directions).
https://www.patheos.com/blogs/religioromana/2013/05/odds-and-evens/

Useful for invoking Janus, which is done a lot. The prayer offered is almost verbatim a section from the oldest Roman ritual invocation (fragmentary of course) still existing:
https://www.patheos.com/blogs/religioromana/2013/01/kalends-rite-for-janus/

http://societasviaromana.net/Collegium_Religionis/contributions.php

One more thing: stay away from Nova Roma. They started as a bunch of atheists more interested in awarding themselves fancy Roman titles and creating a "micronation" of larpers, with Roman Religion just one more part of the larp. Over time that has changed and there might be some legitimate polytheists involved, but I worry that their egregore is permanently tainted along with their reputation. Best to just avoid them altogether and skip the viscious back and forth drama.

replies.title: Re: Hexagram
replies.content:
All the astrological planets have their own spirits, who routinely show up here when their planet is invoked. (That's caused a lot of confusion among overly material-minded UFO believers, among others.) Each planet's spirits have the same general orientation as their planet, as you'd expect, and so solar spirits are luminous, glorious, and life-giving, but you need to be a little careful when dealing with them because humans who interact with them routinely have their egos puff up. The winged figure in tarot trump XIV, Temperance, is considered in French occult writings to be a traditional image of a solar spirit.

Yes, it's always a good omen when you see a spirit of the planet you've invoked. That goes for all planets -- invoke Saturn to help you pay off a debt, say, and if you see one of the lean, dark, somber, shadowy-winged spirits of Saturn, you'll get the help you asked for. (Since it's Saturn, it'll be measured out with a micrometer, but you'll get it.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not so much astrological, but a few sources that might help with grounding thinking about financial history (so as to link to astrological observations, presumably):

- Nick Szabo's "Shelling Out": https://nakamotoinstitute.org/shelling-out/
A fantastic piece on the really long history of "money" as a concept and technology. Szabo is a hardcore libertarian and proto-crypto-currency theorist (he's been accused of, and denied, being Satoshi), so temper his thinking about money with our host's excellent analysis of the Tertiary Economy in The Wealth of Nature.

- David Graeber's Debt: The First 5000 Years
Graeber was an avowed Marxist, and so his thinking will have all of the strengths and weakensses you might expect from that background. Helpful for giving a really deep dive into how debt has been deeply intertwined with formal economies since the Sumerians, even when not seemingly obvious.

- Charlie Munger's Poor Charlie's Almanack
A really useful insight into how Warren Buffett's business partner thinks about understanding the world, including investing. The short answer is that he devotes a lot of time and attention to understanding businesses as Secondary Economy entities and invests when the Tertiary Economy has gotten a bit off in treating their stocks as quasi-monetary tokens.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Sure, but it's not a fast process. You need to develop a fair amount of skill at astral vision -- the sort of thing you'd get via the Golden Dawn practice of working through the elemental tattwas -- and also enough skill at discursive meditation that you can sort out what in your visions comes from your own subconscious and what is external to you. All that'll give you the skills and knowledge you need to do as St.-Yves d'Alveydre did and make the astral journey there. It's not a trip for beginners!

comments.title: Astrology
comments.content:
Dear Mr Greer

I have a question that is covid related, but is about astrology, so I thought it would be Ok for this blog. There has been speculation about a possible mass death scenario in relation to the vaccines. If this was the case then why can’t astrologers pick this up in the movements of the star/planets?

I am sceptical of astrology, but realise that I cannot dismiss it out of hand without putting the work in to understand it. So please forgive me if the answer to this question is obvious. By the way you may be wondering why a sceptic is asking this question. All I can say is that going into the future is like moving into the dark. You search for any source of light that may guide you. In previous years I could be fairly sure that the next year would be pretty much like the previous year. I don’t feel that way about this year. replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content:
There are three answers to your question, and they all apply.

First, astrologers are human beings, and like other human beings they can miss what's right in front of their faces if it conflicts with their wishes and their beliefs. Do you recall the number of astrologers who insisted all through 2016 that Hillary Clinton was sure to win? They let their hopes get in the way of a clear reading of the chart. That's especially easy to do in what's called "mundane astrology" -- the astrology of politics -- because so many people feel so strongly about that. So quite a few astrologers who bought into the vaccine hype and the stuff the corporate media was saying looked at their charts, and read them through rose-colored glasses.

Second, astrology deals in general trends, and it predicts tendencies rather than certainties. A good mundane astrologer says "there's a high risk of war at this time" rather than being more certain than that, and who the war will be with -- that can't always be determined at all. In the same way, a chart may predict very serious trouble for a certain period without indicating what kind of trouble it is. That's just the way astrology is; human beings have free will, and so the influence of the cycles of time can only tell you so much.

Third, some of us have been warning for some time now that something really nasty is being signaled in the stars. You might find this open post of mine worth reading; it's a set of predictions made for Joe Biden's inauguration, which is the most relentlessly bad mundane chart I've ever cast. Looking back, I can see some mistakes of interpretation in it, but most of it's been pretty accurate -- and it predicts a world-class ghastly mess before Biden leaves office. Draw your own conclusions...

comments.title: Bells & Maori War Cries & Freedom Convoys (Oh, my!)
comments.content:
Hi, JMG, I have a couple of questions this week as I continue to explore the possible magical dimensions of the Freedom Convoy movement against fox mandates:

1 - The bell is one tool which is common in many religions and cultures around the world for cultivating holiness and dispelling evil (it is even used in exorcisms, if I recall correctly). The alloy used for the bell is most commonly brass, but it varies considerably. I have been recently looking at the truck air-horn as an “industrial bell”. Its sound seems to be both powerful (literally shaking your innards if you get too close) and consciousness-changing. My first close encounter with such a horn as a child (which was at very close range and was totally unexpected) was so powerful it felt as if my soul was blown right out of my body for an instant: that memory has always stayed with me. Do you have any comments on bells / truck horns from an occult perspective?

2 - This past weekend the Maoris of New Zealand resumed their ancestral war cry/ritual (haka) in front of New Zealand’s parliament buildings. Many non-Maori Kiwis have learned it and are allowed to join in. The haka was done on mass last November as well (video clip here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oSkmG2nMDxs), but nothing at the physical/social level seemed to come of it. However, as soon as I watched the hakas back in November, I felt that it was a ‘wake-up call’ at a time when no group in the Western world appeared to openly show resistance to the wholesale curtailment of freedoms by governments. (By the way, I have witnessed a haka in person in NZ and no video can possibly do it justice). A lot of other people watched the November 2021 hakas. This pas weekend’s hakas were even bigger and more powerful than November’s. The psychological effects of such war-cries are obvious. I am wondering about the haka’s effects from a more magical perspective and whether they may have perhaps helped kick-start the rebellion against government over-reach (which is the Freedom Convoy movement) at a subtle level. Any musings?

Ron M

I’m not a New Zealander, but the relationship of NZ to the Maori is very interesting to me. I don’t know what it’s like on the ground, but there seems to be far more openness to whites/non-Indigenous adopting cultural aspects of the Maori without it being labeled “cultural appropriation.” For example, the prime minister’s child has a Maori middle name despite all-European ancestry, and the PM appears to regularly adopt Maori dress as a form of respect when visiting their political or religious sites or attending Maori events. I can’t imagine a Canadian, US, or even Australian politician doing this without massive outcry and ridicule, plus accusations of cultural appropriation or appeasement, from whichever end of the spectrum.

Is it maybe because NZ is unusual in having only one, mostly homogeneous indigenous group? Or something particular to the Maori?

replies.title: Re: Bells & Maori War Cries & Freedom Convoys (Oh, my!)
replies.content:
New Zealanders in the group may want to pipe in, but in my experience (having visited the country and having studied NZ history a fair bit), I suspect that in part it has to do with the fact that of all the British Colonies, the native people of NZ were treated the least savagely. The Waitangi Treaty, signed in 1840 between the Maoris and British, gives quite a lot of respect and rights to the Maoris. Moreover, the NZ government has a monument (Te Tii memorial) placed on the site of its signing, with the words, in Maori, carved into stone (I was shaking in awe when I passed my hands over the letters, as we have no such equivalent in Canada). Also, the date of treaty’s signing is commemorated as a national holiday – Waitangi Day.

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Bells & Maori War Cries & Freedom Convoys (Oh, my!)
replies.content:
NZer here.

Te Tiriti O Waitangi / The Treaty of Waitangi is a wonderful thing. It gives me hope for the nation's future. Even more wonderful, and often forgotten, is the Declaration of Independence which predates it (1835). That's when various North Island Maori tribes declared NZ to be independent, and sought the protection of the British Crown, both to assist them in ending their own tribal wars (made far worse with muskets), and to protect NZ from other colonial powers, namely the French (but possibly also New South Wales, which technically ruled NZ at the time).

However, the reality of it was, that without a Treaty or some other political agreement, white settlers didn't stand a chance in early NZ. Maori were just too damned good at many things, including fighting, for that to happen.

It all went wrong when the Treaty got broken. It supposedly protected Maori land from unscrupulous sales, by giving the Crown the monopoly on it, but then when Waikato Maori said they didn't want to sell land, the Crown invaded them, with a few thousand imperial troops.

Maori history since then has been about a sometimes desperate fight to get pennies on the dollar back on what was lost, noting, not all tribes lost. Those tribes that didn't fight the Crown did quite well, but that's an accident of geography.

There's still an incredible amount of goodwill to make whatever the 'treaty partnership' is, work.

replies.title: Re: Bells & Maori War Cries & Freedom Convoys (Oh, my!)
replies.content:
NZer here,

The settlement of Aotearoa/New Zealand, was far from perfect, and had a pretty hideous impact on Maori. It was just a bit less hideous than other places, but that doesn't and shouldn't negate the past, and the need to rectify it. It started with the right intent (for most iwi or tribes) with a couple of treaties between England and Maori, which the Crown later broke, leading to a long war, which the Crown, requiring imperial troops, barely won. Maori still lost most of their land, despite protections in the Treaty of Waitangi to ensure that didn't happen.

Maori have a wonderful generosity of spirit, and I think that is what you are referring to here. If its done with respect, understanding, and without commercial reward, then Maori are usually pretty happy for other people to pick up some concepts.

Haka are an interesting thing. There is a growing dislike of 'gratuitous haka', or haka that are performed at the drop of a hat without context or mana (roughly translated as power/authority). In regards to the protest in Wellington outside Parliament, it's interesting to note that no tribal leaders are present (most have denounced it), and so the haka being performed there will likely only have the power of the individual behind it, not the whole tribe, so I think their effect is limited.

How about a bit of wordplay and look at symbolism, if I may?

One type of bell instrument is the carillon. Yet a horn is also often called a clarion. The end of a trumpet is called a bell. Interesting huh? I think so.

There is a lot of lore around bells that is fascinating to study. The big church bells used to receive their own baptisms for one thing. Then there lots of stories about bells protecting a town, etc. The story of Dick Whittington is another. There are many others. I think there is a good bell story too, in one of Lafcadio Hearn's versions of traditional folklore.

To this member of the commentariat it sounds like there is a big clarion call going out into the world. The war cries would also be a part of that.

One thing is for sure those war cries tap into the power of utterance and the element of air. One unique thing about the air element is that air goes everywhere in the atmosphere of earth. So doing air magic, using voice, utterance, blasts from a horn and sound, has the power to not only effect local change, but could I think, be "heard" all around the world.

In tarot there is often depicted a trumpeters for XX Judgment trump. Thus looking at the situation that way it really is Judgment time and a decision must be made.

comments.title: Christo-Pagan Relations with Christocentric Christians (Repost from Levi discussion)
comments.content:
Hello Everyone.... JMG already chimed in on the following over at Ecosophia. This is a repost of something I posted for the Eliphas Levi book discussion, but I thought I'd put it up here too, so anyone who doesn't go over there for the Wonder-full book discussion could have a stab at it. The question is about relating to Christians as an occultist and Christo-Pagan type person.:

Wearing the Mantle of a (formerly?) Dominant Religion

I was curious what other people’s thoughts are about “remaing silent” and what I’ll call “wearing the mantle of a dominant religion”. Levi says to participate or not, “without impiety.”

I have a lot of Christian family and many Christian friends. Because I know the Bible some, better than some who’ve never read it, and not as well as those who’ve devoted themselves to studying it deeply, some people have just thought I was a Christian without me ever saying I was. I went along with it because it’s often easier to blend in this way, rather than be like, well actually, no, I’m a Universalist Occultist/Mage who believes, as far as I can tell, that there is a Divinity, but that there are differences between Divinity and Deity. I see the Divinity as the One, perhaps as a Neoplatonist would, but then below that are all kinds of Deities and a whole ecology and swirl of spiritual beings (including us because the universe is alive, etc.).

Yaweh, Christ, etc. are part of this “world full of gods”. I have a connection to Christ because I was raised Christian. Also had a “Christ acceptance” experience through a member of an esoteric Christian/magical group where I live, but it was a brief association. But “wearing the mantle” of that religion full-time as it were, never did seem right, and there have always been aspects I get the “ick” factor I get from Christianity (no offense meant on any of this to the Christians here).

Yet I share the belief in a “creator”, and I have encountered deep strains of gnosis within the B.I.B.L.E. (Basic Instructions Before Leaving Earth according to the gospel of Wu-Tang). So I always feel with these friends as if I’m skirting around something.

I guess it would be fair to say I could be considered a Christo-Pagan, religiously. But how do Christo-Pagans relate to their Christian friends who would really dislike it if they know they also pray to other gods besides Christ, have a relationship with Elen of the Ways as much as Virgin Mary? And even some Christians would say you’re an idolator if you light a candle at Mary shrine in a local cathedral where you aren’t even part of the congregation. Because Catholics, well they have a pope, and all those shrines and that’s “wrong”.

One of the main problems I’ve always had as someone who was raised in a church that was the “one true religion” even amongst other Christian denominations, is that “believing correctly” was more important than what you did in some cases. Of course you were expected to obey the law – a very legalistic church that kept as many Old Testament rules as they could find. Christians often, very often, point to their interpretations of the Bible as proof of the soundness of their doctrine. But it’s not like the Bible is the only book that contains truth out there… there are many books, and the book of nature and religions that aren’t of the book seem more open to intertextuality and not always thinking theirs is the only true way in the universe. Who is to say the Bible is the only book to be believed (well, Jehovah’s Witnesses say that for example… though I belonged to a different sect/cult).

Anyway, I quit going when I was a teenager, the same time my occult interest ramped up. The numerous splinter groups within Christianity, and many who believed there splinter is the only right one, struck me as hubris. Of course every other non-Christian religion was wrong on top of that, if not actually Satanic, because the deities of other religions are actually demons in disguise. That’s the thinking I’ve encountered more often than not.

And yet I do find truth, with a lower case t, in the Bible. There’s a shale ton of magic in there. A shale ton of mysticism. My magical & some visionary experiences have been proven “true” by Bible verses that I later came to understand in a new “gnostic” way. These have helped me to relate to and accept the Christians in my life in a different way.

Didn’t Levi also something along the lines of one religion for the people, another for the initiate? I’ll have to go back and find it in an earlier chapter.

Are these interactions with devout Christians a place where “to keep silent” is a virtue? I like to have conversations about spirituality with some of them. When it strays into dogma and “believing right” is when I get squirrely. What do others think?

Know Wonder

(Writing under an occasional pseudonym for this comment just in case the Witch Finder Generals ever comes looking for me.)

comments.title: Fear
comments.content: Hello JMG, hope you are doing well on this cold day in the north-east.  I'm interested in your occult perspective on working with fear and anxiety.  I've felt a lot of fear in my life - sensitive nervous system, childhood trauma, family trauma.  It's always been a background shadow in my life, nagging sense of unease. For much of my life I behaved counter-phobically, running at life in spite of the unease I felt, using fear as a thrust block - although I've only learned these concepts later in life - as a teenager and young adult I just did what I did, unconsciously trying to channel all this nervous energy. But this approach also strengthened the sense of fear in me too - the more I tried to escape it the more it hung around in the background, trying to break through more and more.  Later in life I got into meditation, experienced the unease more directly in me and worked to accept it as a part of me, and to smooth the rough edges of it where I could. So, my question, I guess, is what's the appropriate approach to fear - use it as fuel, try to come to terms with it, try to understand it's karmic origins?  At 60 I'm strangely at peace with it now - it's kind of like an irritable lodger in the attic.  But I'm wondering what I might have done differently with it if I had my time again.

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content: I wish I had a good answer. My own experience has been that meditation and the Octagon Society exercises did me a great deal of good, but I have no idea if that's true for everyone.

comments.title: Muscle testing

Obviously that's a big no-no in geomancy, but since in this case the oracle is one's own body, perhaps a different rule applies, as with remote viewing? Or would you advise simply not using muscle testing to divine future and past events (including details about past lives), but only for health diagnoses?

Many thanks as always for Magic Monday!

comments.content:
Hi JMG,

1) Do you think that sex magic has historically been the highest secret in western esotericism, as some allege?

2) What is your opinion of the transgression/antinomianism found in so many groups such as the Sabbateans, the Frankists, the OTO, etc?

3) What do you think of Kenneth Grant?

Thanks again for hosting Magic Mondays!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Not really. During the relatively brief period when frank talk about sex was utterly taboo in most European and European-diaspora countries -- roughly from 1830 to 1920 -- the return of the repressed guaranteed that everyone wanted to talk about sex, and occultists adapted to the changing market. That was also the heyday of the sex cult, which was basically a sex club that pretended to be a religious organization for legal reasons (as well as to give women who belonged a retaliatory weapon against blackmail from men -- to be known as a member of a deviant religious group was just as disastrous to a man's reputation as sexual freedom was to a woman's). So a lot of hanky-panky went on under the guise of occultism.

2) You get that in really repressive cultural settings. It always struck me as rather childish, but whatever floats your boat.

3) He really missed his calling. If he'd set out to make a career as a writer of Lovecraftian horror fiction he'd have earned a worldwide reputation. His magical writings, as magical writings, don't do much for me, but here again, your mileage may vary.

replies.title: Re: Wealth from magic?

You can also do magical workings to stop wasting money on unnecessary things -- most people would be much wealthier than they are if they simply stopped throwing their money away on things they don't actually want, and once you have some spare money, you can use it to make more. 1) I'd encourage you to finish the Lesser Mysteries of the Dolmen Arch at least before you try to adapt the martial arts material, but once you've done that, yes, meditating on it and using divination for guidance would be a good plan.

2) For Druidical acupressure, Jacques de Langre's two books on Do-In are good sources; Do-In 2 is full of Celtic material. The use of Sam (summer) and Giam (winter) as synonyms for yang and yin was introduced by the hugely controversial Neven Henaff, a Breton nationalist who ended up on the wrong side in the Second World War and spent the last part of his life in exile in Ireland; I don't know if anyone else has dared touch those terms since his time.

3) Walter and Litzka Gibson's The Complete Illustrated Book of the Psychic Sciences has full instructions.

4) The Chinese GD system already exists -- or at least there have been rumors to that effect for decades now about a lodge working that system in the San Francisco area. It uses variant pentagram rituals to invoke the five elements and an octagram ritual to invoke the eight trigrams. Druid martial arts also exist -- you might check out Gouren (Breton wrestling_, which is much practiced in the Breton druid scene; it looks like judo with a Celtic accent. There's also been a revival of interest in Irish stick fighting in recent years, but I don't know if Druids have gotten into it yet.

replies.title: Re: MMQ
replies.content: Thank you for both the direction and the resources. I'm really surprised I haven't heard of either of (4). In any case, this is much appreciated.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Not quite. The solar and telluric currents are specific patterns of life force that are generated by the sun and the earth respectively; they correspond to spirit above and spirit below, but these latter are much more general terms. It's like the difference between a river on the one hand, and the element of water on the other.

comments.title: Some questions
comments.content:
1) The Turkey Curse - When is it enough? I've kept on doing it for some time but got a growing feeling that it might become too much if I continue. After all, if I understand this correctly, you are promoting the principle of chaos and some of this jam should stick on your fingers? 2) Gods - Do we know anything about gods that have a special relationship to species other than humans? I'm not very well read in mythology but what I have read so far seems to be either gods expressing "the fabric of the world" like Titans or the Norse Giants, or they seem to be very anthropocentric like the Aesir or the Olympians. Do we know anything about gods that are involved with the countless other species that dwell on this planet?

3) Silence - Is there a point when it's ok to stop being silent on some (occult) business? Like reaching a milestone of some sort? More than once, I have written posts with questions for MM but while writing it down I got the feeling that it's not a good idea to post and it would be better to answer the question myself or not at all. In the sense that "to be silent" is necessary to protect my (very modest) achievements because they are not stable enough. From this insight, I imply that it is possible to reach a plateau of some sorts where you can risk exposing what you're doing without threatening its stability?

Many thanks for your insights and all you're doing here! These days, I feel I have little to contribute but I lurk and read as much as I can and find great value in all that you're doing.

Greetings,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Some questions
replies.content:
1) When it feels like time to stop, stop. That's the only advice I can give you!

2) No doubt there are plenty of them, but since they don't interact with our species, we haven't exactly had much of a chance to learn about them.

3) As far as I know there's never a point where it's a good idea to blabber everything you're doing to all and sundry. Silence is one of the tools of the mage, and a lot of the education of a mage is learning when to use a tool and when to set it aside.

comments.title: Philosophical Museum
comments.content: I'm doing research on a man, Dr. John Murray who lived in Augusta, Ga. in the early 19th Century. I found reference to him being the founder of The Augusta Philosophical Museum. He was born in Nova Scotia and became a Georgia planter later in life. (I'd be curious to know the rest of the story there-He was of the generation that fought in the Revolutionary War.)

replies.title: Re: Philosophical Museum
replies.content: A museum, back in the day, was any organization devoted to the Muses -- that is, to literature, history, and the arts. If his museum was like some of the others in that time, it would have had a library and a meeting room for lectures and discussions, and people joined and paid an annual fee to cover its costs. It might have had an occult dimension -- "philosophical" was sometimes a code word for "occult" in those days -- or it could simply have been for general self-education in the arts and sciences.

In his podcast on 10 Feb 2022 (The Spiritual Void in the West), Jordan Peterson laments the loss of shamanic traditions in traditional Western Christianity. He thinks it would be beneficial to have a mystery religion component to Christianity, such as the Eleusinian Mysteries. He admits he has no idea of how to go about it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
What differentiates a mystery religion from the usual kind is that mystery religions start from the recognition that religion is an experience, not an ideology, and that spiritual realities cannot be expressed in any dogmatic, doctrinal, or ideological form -- only in the forms of myth and ritual. A Christian mystery religion wouldn't worry about who had what opinions; its attitude would be, "If you want to experience our ritual and participate in the reenactment of our myths, you're welcome."

That's what the Eleusinian mysteries were about. You didn't have to believe that the story of Demeter and Persephone actually happened in the crass film-at-eleven sense. You simply had to accept that story as an expression of a spiritual truth, and take part in the ceremonies in a reverent and attentive manner.

I don't see any likelihood that Christians are prepared to approach their faith that way, but I admit I'd be glad to be wrong.
